region_of_origin: China; widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general goose meat food product entry; no specific roasted-with-skin variant exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers goose meat broadly; no roasted-with-skin sub-facet available. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for roasted goose with skin. Null used to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted goose with skin is approximately 150g, representing a moderate portion of mixed cuts including breast and thigh meat with attached skin.

nutrient_source.title: Goose, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 05143); supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo Database (cr_id F11050003)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central; Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient anchor is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 05143 (Goose, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted; FDC ID 172288), which is an exact match for this food. This source provides energy, protein, fat, saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol values. The HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id F11050003, confidence 0.95) was used as a corroborating regional source and confirms the same core macro values. Where HPB and USDA values align, USDA is treated as the primary citation for completeness. Saturated fat (6.87g/100g), monounsaturated fat (9.78g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (2.59g/100g) are from USDA FDC ID 172288.

gi_source.title: N/A — Roasted goose with skin contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — not applicable for zero-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted goose with skin contains 0g carbohydrates per 100g as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 05143 and HPB Foodo cr_id F11050003. Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and does not list a GI value for roasted goose, consistent with the food being a negligible-carbohydrate protein-and-fat product. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.87g/100g exceeds the AHA 5g/100g threshold, contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium is low at 70mg/100g and potassium is favourable at 329mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should remove the skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake below the AHA threshold, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces or glazes that would elevate the overall sodium load of the meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar with high protein at 25.16g/100g; no glycaemic impact, but saturated fat at 6.87g/100g may contribute to insulin resistance with frequent consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted goose with skin is suitable as a protein source for blood glucose management given its zero carbohydrate content, but individuals with type 2 diabetes should remove the skin to limit saturated fat intake and avoid sweet glazes or hoisin-based sauces that add carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 21.92g/100g with saturated fat 6.87g/100g and cholesterol 91mg/100g; high caloric density at 305 kcal/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume roasted goose with skin in moderation, preferring skinless portions to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol load, and pairing with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate LDL-raising effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.87g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold; goose skin does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA carve-out
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should remove the skin before eating to reduce saturated fat below the RA threshold, as elevated saturated fat promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity that worsens joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted goose with skin is a whole goose that has been dry-heat roasted until fully cooked through, yielding a rich golden-brown to deep mahogany exterior with a crispy, rendered skin and succulent, flavourful meat beneath. The dish is a staple of Chinese culinary tradition, particularly associated with Cantonese roast meat culture, and is widely consumed in Singapore, Hong Kong, and across Southeast Asia. The meat is rich and slightly gamey compared to chicken or duck, with a tender texture across breast, thigh, and drumstick cuts. Per 100g, roasted goose with skin provides 305 kcal, 25.16g protein, 21.92g total fat (including approximately 6.87g saturated fat, 9.78g monounsaturated fat, and 2.59g polyunsaturated fat), and 0g carbohydrates, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food with significant fat content concentrated in the skin. Sodium is low at 70mg/100g, while potassium is 329mg/100g and iron is 2.83mg/100g. Clinically, the food is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns and poses no glycaemic concern. However, the saturated fat content from the skin warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Removing the skin substantially reduces the saturated fat burden. Commonly served with steamed rice, pickled mustard greens, or plum sauce in Singaporean and Cantonese contexts.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 05143 (Goose, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted; FDC ID 172288), which is an exact match for this food and an approved primary source. HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11050003, confidence 0.95) corroborates the core macro values. Both are approved sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 05143 and HPB Foodo cr_id F11050003 — roasted goose with skin contains no carbohydrates by composition; this is naturally zero for a roasted meat product. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per the same sources. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure animal protein and fat product with no plant-derived components. Trans_fat_g set to 0.0 as no trans fat is reported for this food in USDA FDC ID 172288. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values were not incorporated in this pass to maintain source granularity consistency; a dedicated micronutrient enrichment pass using USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 172288 is recommended. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per the zero-carbohydrate GI rule. Halal status not confirmed — goose is permissible in Islam but preparation method and slaughter certification are not documented in the source records.
